meta.title: X152 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Jaguar F-Type X152 engine — from the 2.0 Ingenium P300 to the supercharged V6 and V8 SVR. Real UK costs, honest reliability data. Compare prices from 15 vetted Jaguar specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar F-Type X152 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X152 engine costs between £3,000 and £9,000 supply-only, depending on the engine — the 2.0 Ingenium P300 ranges £3,000–£5,000, while the 5.0 V8 supercharged runs £5,500–£9,000. Fitting adds 10–18 hours of labour at £65–£90/hr. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which Jaguar F-Type X152 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The PT204 2.0-litre Ingenium P300 is the most reliable engine in the F-Type line-up — no supercharger cooling system to manage, no V6 supercharger coupler to wear, and no V8 timing-chain complexity. It's the simplest and most dependable F-Type engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar F-Type X152?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump and coolant leaks on the supercharged V6 (306PS) and V8 (508PS), supercharger coupler wear on the V6, timing chain guide wear on early AJ133 V8s (pre-2013 era), and cooling system issues on the 2.0 Ingenium P300. The V6's supercharger coupler is a well-documented wear item that produces a characteristic rattle at idle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar F-Type X152 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and maintenance history. The 2.0 Ingenium P300 is the most reliable. The V6 supercharged is generally reliable if cooling system preventative maintenance is performed. The 5.0 V8 is the most demanding — early (pre-2013) units have timing chain concerns; post-2013 units are improved but still require meticulous cooling system maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Which Jaguar F-Type X152 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Early (2013–2014) 5.0 V8 supercharged units without documented timing chain work are the highest risk. We also advise caution on any supercharged V6 or V8 without evidence of water pump replacement by 60,000 miles. If you must have a V8, choose a post-2017 unit with comprehensive service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X152 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Persistent coolant loss or overheating (AJ126/AJ133), supercharger rattle at idle (AJ126 coupler wear), timing chain rattle on startup (AJ133), or blue smoke and rough idle on any high-mileage unit are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar F-Type X152 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Jaguar scrap threshold. Against a well-kept £25,000 F-Type, a £6,000 V8 recon is 24% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £15,000 early example, the same recon is 40% — borderline. For sub-£12,000 examples, we'd recommend walking away.

mainEntity.name: How long do Jaguar F-Type X152 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The PT204 2.0 Ingenium can comfortably reach 150,000 miles with routine maintenance. The 306PS V6 supercharged is capable of 120,000+ miles with preventative cooling system work. The 508PS V8 supercharged can exceed 100,000 miles but requires meticulous servicing — timing chain work at 60,000–80,000 miles is recommended on early units.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the pre-facelift and facelift F-Type?

acceptedAnswer.text: The pre-facelift F-Type (2013–2019) offered 3.0 V6 supercharged and 5.0 V8 supercharged options, including the SVR (575 bhp). The 2020 facelift introduced a revised front and rear design, dropped the V6 in most markets, replaced it with the P450 (450 bhp 5.0 V8), and added the R P575 (575 bhp). The 2.0 Ingenium P300 continued. The facelift also brought improved infotainment and revised interior trim.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar F-Type X152 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate, vehicle documentation, and often on a sticker in the driver's door jamb or under the bonnet. For 2.0 petrol, look for PT204 (AJ20P4 — P300). For 3.0 supercharged V6, look for 306PS (AJ126 — V6, V6 S, 400 Sport). For 5.0 supercharged V8, look for 508PS (AJ133/AJ133G2 — V8, R, SVR, P450, P575). Tell us your variant and year, and we'll confirm.

hero.h1: Jaguar F-Type X152 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Jaguar F-Type X152 — from the 2.0-litre Ingenium turbo four to the 3.0-litre supercharged V6 and the fire-breathing 5.0-litre supercharged V8 SVR. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the cooling-system questions that define this generation's ownership experience. Backed by 260+ real UK F-Type enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar F-Type X152 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F-Type X152 specifically — from the 2013 launch through the 2020 facelift and the final 2024 model year.

overview.intro: The Jaguar F-Type X152 is a two-door, two-seater sports car manufactured by Jaguar Land Rover from December 2012 to June 2024 at the Castle Bromwich assembly plant in the UK. Designed by Ian Callum as the spiritual successor to the legendary E-Type, the F-Type was Jaguar's return to a two-seater sports car after almost 40 years. The engine line-up evolved significantly over its 11-year production run: early cars offered 3.0-litre supercharged V6 and 5.0-litre supercharged V8 options; a 2.0-litre Ingenium turbocharged four-cylinder was added in 2017; the 2020 facelift replaced the V6 with a detuned 5.0-litre V8 (P450), leaving the range with just the 2.0-litre four and 5.0-litre V8. The range-topping SVR and Project 7 delivered 575 bhp from the supercharged 5.0-litre V8. In total, 87,731 F-Types were built.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: JLR D6a aluminium architecture • Predecessor: Jaguar XK • Successor: Discontinued (no direct successor) • Sibling body styles: Coupé and Convertible — both share the X152 designation
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The Jaguar F-Type X152 generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025, with the 3.0-litre supercharged V6 (AJ126) accounting for the largest share of the 560 JLR-wide figure, followed by the 5.0-litre supercharged V8 (AJ133) at 330 enquiries and the 2.0-litre Ingenium four (PT204) at 500 enquiries.

items.engine: PT204 2.0-litre Ingenium P300 — The 300 bhp turbocharged four-cylinder is the most reliable engine in the F-Type line-up. No supercharger cooling system to manage, no V8 timing-chain complexity, and a simpler overall architecture than the V6 and V8 options.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want sports car looks with everyday running costs.

items.engine: 508PS 5.0-litre Supercharged V8 (pre-2013 era) — Early AJ133 V8s (2013–2014) suffer from timing-chain guide wear and cooling system leaks. Revised in 2013+ models with significantly more robust timing components. The 5.0 V8 is a high-performance engine that demands meticulous maintenance.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets and a commitment to specialist servicing.

items.engine: AJ133 5.0-litre Supercharged V8 (SVR/R) — The 575 bhp SVR V8 is the most expensive engine in the F-Type line-up to replace, with reconditioned units costing £5,500–£9,000 supply-only. Timing-chain guide wear and cooling system leaks are the primary risks. A new engine from Jaguar is approximately £15,500 + VAT.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts and collectors who accept the premium.

items.engine: PT204 2.0-litre Ingenium P300 — The 300 bhp four-cylinder offers the best balance of performance, running costs, and reliability. The cheapest recon cost in the range and the simplest architecture make this the most affordable F-Type to own.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise running costs over outright performance.

items.engine: 508PS 5.0-litre Supercharged V8 P450 (post-2020 facelift) — Often overlooked by buyers who default to the SVR or the V6, the 450 bhp V8 P450 delivers genuine V8 character with lower running costs than the 575 bhp SVR. Post-2020 units benefit from revised timing components and improved cooling system design.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 presence without the SVR's premium.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Water pump, coolant leaks, supercharger coupler
rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Choice — cooling system is watch-item

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain guide wear, coolant leaks

rows.ownershipVerdict: Too Early — no major pattern yet

economicsRule.title: The X152 rule
economicsRule.text: A £5,500–£9,000 V8 recon against a £15,000–£20,000 early F-Type sits at 28–60% of the car's value. On a sub-£15,000 example, that brushes our 60% Jaguar threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £25,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 22–36% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X152 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: PT204 2.0-litre P300 — cheapest recon, simplest architecture.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X152 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 508PS 5.0-litre V8 SVR — timing-chain and cooling-system repairs are expensive.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X152 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 306PS 3.0-litre V6 S (380 bhp) — the sweet spot between performance, sound, and running costs.

problems.title: AJ126/AJ133 Supercharged V6/V8 Cooling System Failure
problems.affectedModels: X152 3.0 SCV6 (306PS) and 5.0 SCV8 (508PS), all years.
problems.rootCause: Water pump failure and coolant leaks from crossover pipes are well-documented on both supercharged V6 and V8 petrols. The supercharger sits in the engine's 'V', making access to cooling components labour-intensive. Coolant loss can lead to overheating and, if ignored, catastrophic engine failure.
problems.recommendation: On any X152 SCV6 or SCV8 approaching 60,000 miles, replace the water pump as preventative maintenance — £600–£1,600 now is cheaper than the £4,500+ engine replacement that follows an overheat.

cta.label: Compare 306PS/508PS engine prices for your F-Type →

problems.title: AJ133 5.0 V8 Timing Chain Guide Wear
problems.affectedModels: X152 5.0 SCV8 (508PS), particularly pre-2013 era engines.
problems.rootCause: Timing chain guides on early AJ133 V8s can wear, leading to chain stretch and, in catastrophic cases, valve damage. Revised in 2013+ models with significantly more robust timing components. The issue is less prevalent on post-2013 units but remains a watch-item on high-mileage examples.
problems.recommendation: On any X152 V8, have the timing chain inspected at 60,000 miles — a £2,500–£4,500 preventative repair is cheaper than the £5,500–£9,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare 508PS engine prices for your F-Type →

problems.title: AJ126 3.0 V6 Supercharger Coupler Wear
problems.rootCause: The supercharger coupler (the nylon/plastic coupling between the supercharger and the drive pulley) can wear over time, leading to a rattling noise from the supercharger at idle. While not immediately catastrophic, ignored wear can lead to supercharger failure.
problems.recommendation: If your X152 V6 exhibits a rattling noise from the supercharger at idle, have the coupler inspected immediately — £500–£1,000 now is cheaper than the £3,000+ supercharger replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare 306PS supercharger repair prices for your F-Type →

replacementCosts.h2: X152 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X152 F-Type only. Engine fitment varies by year — the 2.0-litre Ingenium P300 was added in 2017; the 2020 facelift introduced the P450 (detuned 5.0 V8) and R P575, while discontinuing the V6 option in most markets. The SVR (575 bhp) was introduced in 2015 and continued through 2020. The Project 7 (575 bhp, limited to 250 units) was produced in 2015.

mostCommonFailures: 3. AJ133 timing chain guide wear (pre-2013 era)
